edges.description: Chronic ischemia and oligemia are proposed to drive diffuse proliferative vascular remodeling.
Diffuse vascular shunting and hemodynamic delay can disturb cerebral blood flow.
edges.target: Altered cerebral perfusion and ischemic symptoms

edges.description: Diffuse proangiogenic CPA biology may promote local VEGF-associated adjacent tissue remodeling.

nodes.description: CPA can alter cerebral hemodynamics, producing focal deficits, headache, and seizures; pediatric cases often present with ischemia and diffuse eloquent-intermixed vasculature.
nodes.id: Altered cerebral perfusion and ischemic symptoms

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral proliferative angiopathy in pediatric patients: case-based review with an illustrative case.
evidence.snippet: Pediatric CPA often manifests with ischemia and diffuse, eloquent-intermixed vasculature.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluating Vascular Hemodynamics in Cerebral Proliferative Angiopathy Using Advanced Arterial Spin Labeling MRI.
evidence.snippet: Cerebral proliferative angiopathy (CPA) is a rare vascular disorder that affects normal blood circulation in the brain.

nodes.description: CPA is hypothesized to arise from reduced perinidal perfusion and chronic cortical ischemia, which promotes diffuse angiogenesis and recruitment of abnormal arteriovenous channels.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral proliferative angiopathy: clinical and angiographic description of an entity different from cerebral AVMs.
evidence.snippet: The diffuse angiogenetic activity is presumably related to reduced perinidal perfusion and subsequent chronic cortical ischemia.

nodes.description: CPA has a diffuse angioarchitecture with intermingled brain parenchyma and no compact nidus, distinguishing it from classic arteriovenous malformation. The malformation alters local cerebral circulation and can involve eloquent brain regions.

evidence.reference_title: A potential VEGF-driven hypothesis of calvarial centripetal proliferation in cerebral proliferative angiopathy.
evidence.snippet: Cerebral proliferative angiopathy (CPA) is a rare subtype of cerebral arteriovenous malformation, characterized by unique angiographic features and clinical presentations.

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent Intraventricular Haemorrhage in Cerebral Proliferative Angiopathy - A Case Report and Review of the Literature.
evidence.snippet: Angioarchitectural workup revealed intermingled brain parenchyma between vascular spaces, absence of dominant feeders and a clear nidus consistent with CPA.

nodes.description: Some patients have calvarial thickening adjacent to CPA. A proangiogenic mechanism involving elevated VEGF has been proposed as a hypothesis linking CPA biology to surrounding tissue remodeling.

evidence.reference_title: A potential VEGF-driven hypothesis of calvarial centripetal proliferation in cerebral proliferative angiopathy.
evidence.snippet: We hypothesized that this may be related to elevated vascular endothelial growth factor levels due to the proangiogenic nature of CPA.
